examples.input: Person has Alice; Address missing for Alice

-- Combine Person with Address (keep people without addresses)

FROM Person p

Table Employee(id INT, salary INT).
Write a query to return the second highest distinct salary as SecondHighestSalary.
If there is no second highest, return NULL (one row).
constraints: Return NULL if fewer than 2 distinct values

starter.sql:
-- Return second highest distinct salary (NULL if none)
SELECT

problems.title: Employees Earning More Than Managers
problems.prompt:
Table Employee(id INT, name VARCHAR, salary INT, managerId INT).
managerId is NULL for top-level employees.
Find employees who earn strictly more than their manager. Return the employee name as Employee.

starter.sql:
-- Employees with salary > manager salary
SELECT
FROM Employee e

solution.sql:
SELECT e.name AS Employee
FROM Employee e
JOIN Employee m ON e.managerId = m.id
WHERE e.salary > m.salary;

problems.title: Duplicate Emails
problems.prompt:
Table Person(id INT, email VARCHAR).
Write a query to find all duplicate emails (emails appearing more than once). Return column Email.

starter.sql:
-- Emails that appear more than once
SELECT
FROM Person

solution.sql:
SELECT email AS Email
FROM Person
GROUP BY email
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

problems.title: Customers Who Never Order
problems.prompt:
Tables:
Customers(id INT, name VARCHAR)
Orders(id INT, customerId INT)

Return customers who never placed an order. Output column Customers (the customer name).

examples.input: Customer Joe has no Orders rows

constraints: NOT IN / LEFT JOIN anti-join / NOT EXISTS

starter.sql:
-- Customers with zero orders
SELECT

solution.sql:
SELECT c.name AS Customers
FROM Customers c
LEFT JOIN Orders o ON c.id = o.customerId
WHERE o.id IS NULL;

samples.input: Customer Joe has no Orders rows

problems.title: Rising Temperature
problems.prompt:
Table Weather(id INT, recordDate DATE, temperature INT).
Find ids where temperature is higher than the previous calendar day's temperature.
Return id.
constraints: Self-join on date = date - 1 day (or LAG)

starter.sql:
-- Ids hotter than yesterday
SELECT
FROM Weather w

solution.sql:
SELECT w.id
FROM Weather w
JOIN Weather y
  ON w.recordDate = DATE_ADD(y.recordDate, INTERVAL 1 DAY)
WHERE w.temperature > y.temperature;
-- PostgreSQL alternative: w.recordDate = y.recordDate + INTERVAL '1 day'

problems.title: Classes More Than 5 Students
problems.prompt:
Table Courses(student VARCHAR, class VARCHAR).
Each row means a student is enrolled in a class.
Return classes with at least 5 distinct students. Column class.
constraints: GROUP BY class HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT student) >= 5

starter.sql:
-- Classes with >= 5 students
SELECT class
FROM Courses
GROUP BY class
HAVING

solution.sql:
SELECT class
FROM Courses
GROUP BY class
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT student) >= 5;

problems.title: Employee Bonus Below Threshold
problems.prompt:
Tables:
Employee(empId INT, name VARCHAR, supervisor INT, salary INT)
Bonus(empId INT, bonus INT)

Return employee name and bonus for employees with bonus < 1000 OR no bonus row.
Columns: name, bonus.

starter.sql:
-- Employees with bonus < 1000 or missing bonus
SELECT
FROM Employee e
LEFT JOIN Bonus b ON e.empId = b.empId
WHERE

solution.sql:
SELECT e.name, b.bonus
FROM Employee e
LEFT JOIN Bonus b ON e.empId = b.empId
WHERE b.bonus < 1000 OR b.bonus IS NULL;

problems.title: Rank Scores
problems.prompt:
Table Scores(id INT, score DECIMAL).
Rank scores from highest to lowest with these rules:
- Same scores share the same rank
- After a tie, the next rank is the next consecutive integer (no holes) — use DENSE_RANK semantics
Return columns score and rank, ordered by score descending.

starter.sql:
-- Rank scores (dense, no holes after ties)
SELECT score,
       DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY score DESC) AS `rank`
FROM Scores
-- ORDER BY ...

solution.sql:
SELECT score,
       DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY score DESC) AS `rank`
FROM Scores
ORDER BY score DESC;

problems.title: Consecutive Numbers
problems.prompt:
Table Logs(id INT, num INT) with ids consecutive.
Find numbers that appear at least three times consecutively (three rows in a row with same num).
Return Distinct ConsecutiveNums.
constraints: Self-join id, id+1, id+2 or window

starter.sql:
-- Numbers appearing 3 times in a row
SELECT DISTINCT
FROM Logs l1

solution.sql:
SELECT DISTINCT l1.num AS ConsecutiveNums
FROM Logs l1
JOIN Logs l2 ON l1.id = l2.id - 1 AND l1.num = l2.num
JOIN Logs l3 ON l1.id = l3.id - 2 AND l1.num = l3.num;

problems.title: Department Highest Salary
problems.prompt:
Tables:
Employee(id INT, name VARCHAR, salary INT, departmentId INT)
Department(id INT, name VARCHAR)

Find employees with the highest salary in each department.
Return Department, Employee, Salary.

starter.sql:
-- Top salary employee(s) per department
SELECT
FROM Employee e
JOIN Department d ON e.departmentId = d.id
WHERE

solution.sql:
SELECT d.name AS Department, e.name AS Employee, e.salary AS Salary
FROM Employee e
JOIN Department d ON e.departmentId = d.id
WHERE (e.departmentId, e.salary) IN (
  SELECT departmentId, MAX(salary)
  FROM Employee
  GROUP BY departmentId
);

problems.title: Managers With At Least 5 Reports
problems.prompt:
Table Employee(id INT, name VARCHAR, department VARCHAR, managerId INT).
Return names of managers who have at least 5 direct reports.
constraints: GROUP BY managerId HAVING COUNT(*) >= 5 then join names

starter.sql:
-- Managers with >= 5 direct reports
SELECT name
FROM Employee
WHERE id IN (

solution.sql:
SELECT name
FROM Employee
WHERE id IN (
  SELECT managerId
  FROM Employee
  WHERE managerId IS NOT NULL
  GROUP BY managerId
  HAVING COUNT(*) >= 5
);

FROM Activity

problems.title: Customer Who Bought All Products
problems.prompt:
Tables:
Customer(customer_id INT, product_key INT)
Product(product_key INT) — complete product catalog

Report customer_ids who bought every product in Product (distinct product_key coverage).

starter.sql:
-- Customers who bought the entire catalog
SELECT customer_id
FROM Customer
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING

solution.sql:
SELECT customer_id
FROM Customer
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT product_key) = (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Product);

problems.title: Tree Node Type
problems.prompt:
Table Tree(id INT, p_id INT). Each node has id; root has p_id NULL.
Label each node as 'Root', 'Inner', or 'Leaf':
- Root: p_id IS NULL
- Leaf: not root and never appears as p_id
- Inner: otherwise
Return id, type.

examples.input: 1 root, 2 child of 1, no children under 2

starter.sql:
-- Classify tree nodes
SELECT id,
CASE
WHEN p_id IS NULL THEN 'Root'
WHEN id NOT IN (SELECT p_id FROM Tree WHERE p_id IS NOT NULL) THEN 'Leaf'
ELSE 'Inner'
END AS type
FROM Tree;

solution.sql:
SELECT id,
CASE
  WHEN p_id IS NULL THEN 'Root'
  WHEN id NOT IN (SELECT p_id FROM Tree WHERE p_id IS NOT NULL) THEN 'Leaf'
  ELSE 'Inner'
END AS type
FROM Tree;

samples.input: 1 root, 2 child of 1, no children under 2

problems.title: Confirmation Rate
problems.prompt:
Tables:
Signups(user_id INT, time_stamp DATETIME)
Confirmations(user_id INT, time_stamp DATETIME, action ENUM('confirmed','timeout'))

Confirmation rate = confirmed / total confirmation requests (0 if no requests).
Return user_id, confirmation_rate rounded to 2 decimals.

starter.sql:
-- Per-user confirmation rate
SELECT s.user_id,
ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN c.action = 'confirmed' THEN 1.0 ELSE 0 END), 2) AS confirmation_rate
FROM Signups s
LEFT JOIN Confirmations c ON s.user_id = c.user_id
GROUP BY s.user_id;

solution.sql:
SELECT s.user_id,
ROUND(COALESCE(AVG(CASE WHEN c.action = 'confirmed' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END), 0), 2) AS confirmation_rate
FROM Signups s
LEFT JOIN Confirmations c ON s.user_id = c.user_id
GROUP BY s.user_id;

problems.title: Monthly Transactions I
problems.prompt:
Table Transactions(id INT, country VARCHAR, state ENUM('approved','declined'), amount INT, trans_date DATE).
For each month and country, return: month (YYYY-MM), country, trans_count, approved_count, trans_total_amount, approved_total_amount.

starter.sql:
-- Monthly transaction stats by country
SELECT
FROM Transactions
GROUP BY 1, 2;

solution.sql:
SELECT DATE_FORMAT(trans_date, '%Y-%m') AS month,
       country,
       COUNT(*) AS trans_count,
       SUM(state = 'approved') AS approved_count,
       SUM(amount) AS trans_total_amount,
       SUM(IF(state = 'approved', amount, 0)) AS approved_total_amount
FROM Transactions
GROUP BY month, country;
-- PostgreSQL: to_char(trans_date, 'YYYY-MM'), FILTER (WHERE state = 'approved')

problems.title: Department Top Three Salaries
problems.prompt:
Tables:
Employee(id INT, name VARCHAR, salary INT, departmentId INT)
Department(id INT, name VARCHAR)

A company's executives are interested in the top three unique salaries in each department.
Return Department, Employee, Salary for employees who earn among the top three unique salaries in their department.

starter.sql:
-- Top 3 unique salaries per department
SELECT
FROM (
  SELECT d.name AS Department, e.name AS Employee, e.salary AS Salary,
         DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY e.departmentId ORDER BY e.salary DESC) AS rnk
  FROM Employee e
  JOIN Department d ON e.departmentId = d.id
) t
WHERE rnk <= 3;

solution.sql:
SELECT Department, Employee, Salary
FROM (
  SELECT d.name AS Department,
         e.name AS Employee,
         e.salary AS Salary,
         DENSE_RANK() OVER (
           PARTITION BY e.departmentId
           ORDER BY e.salary DESC
         ) AS rnk
  FROM Employee e
  JOIN Department d ON e.departmentId = d.id
) ranked
WHERE rnk <= 3;

problems.title: Trips and Users Cancellation
problems.prompt:
Tables:
Trips(id INT, client_id INT, driver_id INT, city_id INT, status ENUM('completed','cancelled_by_driver','cancelled_by_client'), request_at DATE)
Users(users_id INT, banned VARCHAR('Yes','No'), role ENUM('client','driver','partner'))

Cancellation rate = uncompleted trips / total trips for unbanned clients AND unbanned drivers, per day, for dates '2013-10-01' to '2013-10-03'.
Round to 2 decimals. Return Day, Cancellation Rate.
constraints: Filter banned users, date range, ROUND

starter.sql:
-- Daily cancellation rate for unbanned users
SELECT
FROM Trips t

solution.sql:
SELECT t.request_at AS Day,
ROUND(
  SUM(t.status != 'completed') / COUNT(*),
  2
) AS `Cancellation Rate`
FROM Trips t
JOIN Users c ON t.client_id = c.users_id AND c.banned = 'No' AND c.role = 'client'
JOIN Users d ON t.driver_id = d.users_id AND d.banned = 'No' AND d.role = 'driver'
WHERE t.request_at BETWEEN '2013-10-01' AND '2013-10-03'
GROUP BY t.request_at;

problems.title: Human Traffic of Stadium
problems.prompt:
Table Stadium(id INT, visit_date DATE, people INT) with consecutive ids.
Display records with people >= 100 for three or more consecutive ids/rows.
Return id, visit_date, people ordered by visit_date.

examples.input: ids 1..7 with people pattern crossing 100 for 3+ streak

starter.sql:
-- Rows in streaks of 3+ days with people >= 100
SELECT DISTINCT s1.id, s1.visit_date, s1.people
FROM Stadium s1, Stadium s2, Stadium s3
WHERE s1.people >= 100 AND s2.people >= 100 AND s3.people >= 100
  AND (
    (s1.id = s2.id - 1 AND s2.id = s3.id - 1) OR
    (s1.id = s2.id + 1 AND s2.id = s3.id + 1) OR
    (s1.id = s2.id - 1 AND s1.id = s3.id + 1)
  )
ORDER BY s1.visit_date;

solution.sql:
WITH filtered AS (
  SELECT id, visit_date, people,
         id - ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY id) AS grp
  FROM Stadium
  WHERE people >= 100
), streaks AS (
  SELECT grp
  FROM filtered
  GROUP BY grp
  HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3
)
SELECT f.id, f.visit_date, f.people
FROM filtered f
JOIN streaks s ON f.grp = s.grp
ORDER BY f.visit_date;

samples.input: ids 1..7 with people pattern crossing 100 for 3+ streak

problems.title: Exchange Seats
problems.prompt:
Table Seat(id INT PK, student VARCHAR) with consecutive ids starting at 1.
Swap every two consecutive students' seats. If the number of students is odd, the last student is not swapped.
Return id, student ordered by id.
constraints: CASE on id % 2, careful with last odd id

starter.sql:
-- Swap adjacent seats; last odd stays
SELECT
CASE
  WHEN id % 2 = 1 AND id = (SELECT MAX(id) FROM Seat) THEN id
  WHEN id % 2 = 1 THEN id + 1
  ELSE id - 1
END AS id,
student
FROM Seat
ORDER BY id;

solution.sql:
SELECT
  CASE
    WHEN id % 2 = 1 AND id = (SELECT MAX(id) FROM Seat) THEN id
    WHEN id % 2 = 1 THEN id + 1
    ELSE id - 1
  END AS id,
  student
FROM Seat
ORDER BY id;

problems.title: Find Median Given Frequency
problems.prompt:
Table Numbers(num INT, frequency INT) where frequency is how many times num appears in a multiset.
Find the median of the multiset. If even count, return average of two middle values (as float).
Return median.

examples.input: nums expanded by frequency; odd total

constraints: Expand frequencies conceptually via windows / cumulative frequency

starter.sql:
-- Median from value frequencies
-- Interview approach: cumulative frequency then pick middle
WITH ordered AS (
  SELECT num, frequency,
         SUM(frequency) OVER (ORDER BY num) AS cum_freq,
         SUM(frequency) OVER () AS total
  FROM Numbers
)
SELECT AVG(num * 1.0) AS median
FROM ordered
WHERE cum_freq >= (total + 1) / 2
  AND cum_freq - frequency < (total + 2) / 2;

solution.sql:
WITH ordered AS (
  SELECT num,
         frequency,
         SUM(frequency) OVER (ORDER BY num) AS cum_freq,
         SUM(frequency) OVER () AS total
  FROM Numbers
)
SELECT AVG(num * 1.0) AS median
FROM ordered
WHERE cum_freq >= FLOOR((total + 1) / 2)
  AND cum_freq - frequency < CEIL((total + 1) / 2.0);

samples.input: nums expanded by frequency; odd total
